segments.text: and the team that have come in. Welcome to-
They're still coming.
Yeah, they're still coming through the door.
You're our first minister that we've got here in DNA this morning, so
great to have you with us for an hour.
And I know that you will likely have an
introduction for us, a short introduction, and then I will open up for questions.
So, I'll hand over to you.
Fabulous. Thank you, Madam Chair. Well, good morning, everybody.
And thank you for having me here this morning, as well as all my
officials. And I expect, given this is an operational department, we'll also
be relying heavily on their individual expertise.
As Minister of Internal Affairs, I oversee a department that
supports New Zealanders throughout their lives.
I expect it to do so efficiently, with careful cost
management and a clear focus on delivering its core
responsibilities. The Internal Affairs portfolio is
broad. It includes identity and life event
services, our national collections through Archives New
Zealand and the National Library, regulatory functions, and
support for major inquiries. I am also responsible for
appropriations within Vote Internal Affairs.
I'm also responsible for two Crown entities, Fire Emergency New
Zealand and the Classifications Office.
And from the 1st of July this year, one Crown entity
called Safety Net Critical Communications This includes
reviewing their performance, participating in the setting and monitoring of their
strategic direction and targets, and making appointments to their
boards.
I'm pleased to report a strong start to this year, with my
ministerial portfolio progressing a number of key
legislative priorities. We have passed the Online
Casino Gambling Act alongside the associated
regulations, as well as the Regulatory Systems
Amendment Act. I also acknowledge the role of this
committee in that work.
I'm sure there was a lot of work that went into it, and so I very much appreciate
your diligence over those matters.
Since becoming minister, I've been pleased to see faster, more
efficient services being delivered.
New Zealanders are now receiving their passports in around three
working days, down from 27 days of
waiting when I came into office,
and receiving citizenship in about 50 working days,
down from a wait time of 180 days.
These processing times reflect sustained improvement in
performance.
The department continues to deliver value through its stewardship
of New Zealand's knowledge and history.
I recently, this year, launched ANZAC Stories, a new
digital learning platform designed to help young people
across the country explore ANZAC
histories. This platform was developed by Archives
New Zealand and the National Library, using national
collections to make these stories more accessible to future
generations. I recently opened the Heritage
Campus on 25 March this year, and the new
archives building, Te Rua, was delivered on time and
under budget. I've continued to champion work to strengthen
key systems and services across this portfolio.
I've also taken further action on public lending rights, proposing
amendments to regulations to improve that scheme.
These updates aim to reduce administrative burden for both authors
and the National Library, and simplify access to payments for
authors. The department has made good progress in a disciplined
fiscal environment, improving performance and focusing on its core
role, and I'm confident that will continue this year.
Thank you, Madam Chair, and I welcome-
Thank you
... questions from the committee.
Thank you, Minister. And I have first question from Mike, then Lisa.
Thank you, Minister. I just have
a question on the online casino gambling, because
there's obviously been a significant decrease in funding
from over six million to two million, and see the bill's
passed and about to go into the auction process.
I'm just wondering if there's sufficient
funding to actually do that process justice.
So, the online casino gambling legislation and the
regulations put in place the scheme for the licensing
of online gambling.
The functions of that regulator will be funded
through levies directly onto the licensed providers,
rather than coming directly from core funding.
So, I don't expect there to be any difficulty with
overseeing the licensing process, because that will be
directly funded by the expressions of interest
process as well as the ongoing license year
on year.
And will online casino operators that were
operating with New Zealand prior to that coming in
place, will they be excluded from the auction process?
That is not my understanding. There
shouldn't be anything
prohibiting somebody putting forward an expressions of interest.
It is simply a question of whether or not they meet the thresholds
for the license itself, whether they meet the standards that the
department and the secretary expect of them.
But there is no barrier to somebody putting forward an expression of interest,
other than the $19,000, I believe, that needs to be stumped
up for an expression.
But weren't they operating illegally in New Zealand prior to
this act? And do they have the social license, I guess, to then
put an application
in?
Well, the difficulty here is
we do actually want to have a competitive market, and it's
not for me as a minister to say that any one particular person will be precluded,
given that I don't actually know who's going to be putting themselves forward.
It's possible that only people who put themselves forward have never
acted in the New Zealand market prior to this point, but
it's also possible that the only people that we have coming forward are people who
have acted in this space, and there won't be any new players.
It is really a question for the secretary as to whether or not they
believe that the certain license conditions have been met as part of that
expressions of interest process and the licensing auction.
But it's not for me as a minister to preclude and say any particular person
is in or out of the system at this point in time.
Chair.
Very quick supplement. Thank you, Minister, for your opening comments.
In terms of, and you've just alluded to the last question in terms of the Secretary
of Internal Affairs, but I just wanted to ask, is your expectation, or can you
explain the suitability or assessment criteria of those
licensees have
in terms of the budget? Will there be any work in terms of that?
Yes, I can ask our official here to go into more
detail on the licensing process in terms of what sort of
standards may be required of any license
as part of the auction.
Mōrena. John Sneed, Acting Dep Sec
of Regulatory and Identity. So, there's effectively a two-stage assessment
process. The first is an expression of interest process.
There will be a high-level assessment of suitability, meeting some
pretty high-level criteria around, for example,
association with
Organised crime, for example. And anyone who
passes that stage will be through the competitive auction process.
Following the competitive auction process, which they'll
they'll be assessed against a range of criteria, which will include their harm
minimization standards, their age verification standards, consumer
protection standards, basic minimum technical standards to ensure security and
integrity of the information they hold on the platform.
So coming out of the auction process, there's still actually quite
a significant assessment process against those criteria before someone will be
licensed.
Thank you. And just quickly.
Yeah.
And so the question will be, is the
enough to cover those processes in terms of starting up the
system?
We're comfortable that the
funding system that's been set up is going to be able to deliver sufficient
resources to the department to oversee these.
Thank you.
And Andy Hazell, sir.
Yeah, so just on the criteria. So once you've got through
the first hurdle, you're not involved with crime
or anything like that. But the criteria, and just to follow up with Mike's question
as well. The
assertion that people are operating illegally, my understanding is that, in fact,
they can't have been operating illegally because there was no law saying you
couldn't operate. There is now a law which says that you can't operate
without having a license, so they would be operating illegally if they were to
operate without a license going forward.
But at the moment, in the past, they have not been because there has not been a law
prohibiting it. Correct?
That's correct.
Yeah.
It was unregulated.
Right. Okay. So the area of question I just wanted to test was, so once
we got through that first hurdle, the competitive auction, so clearly there's a
whole range of different
criteria. It's not just simply who's going to give the most money.
One of the criteria we did put in there was the New Zealand connection.
There's one you didn't mention. I just wanted to check that that was actually going
to be-
Yeah
... because it's now a requirement in the law.
So a New Zealand connection is at least they say whether they've got one or
haven't.
And also that that's required to be considered.
It doesn't mean it's a determinative factor, but it is required to be considered.
Look, I need to double-check and come back to the committee on that specific
requirement. Certainly, there is a requirement for, for example, to have a New
Zealand registered address so that we have-
Yeah
... have the opportunity to communicate with them.
In terms of that specific point, I'll get back.
It was things like, do they have people based in New Zealand in terms of
employment? Do they have sort of benefit to New Zealand in terms of
beyond 4%, perhaps those kind of issues.
Right.
Yeah. There's no requirement to have a long-standing connection to
New Zealand.
It's not a requirement, it's a consideration.
Mm-hmm.
But the
department is required to have that cons- to give consideration to that.
Doesn't mean they've got to have it, but it's just a factor.
Understood.
Along with all the other ones you mentioned. Yeah.
Yeah. I mean, the difficulty of weighing up
long-standing connection versus being based offshore,
the important thing here is that they have an ability to be contacted in
an online world for the regulations.
There's also another difficulty here of if we restricted it to physically
based-
That's not what I'm saying
... New Zealand platforms-
That's not what I'm saying
... you'd significantly reduce the market.
Yeah.
So I'm not actually sure what your question is really pointing to.
No, my question was there was a range of criteria as well as simply the financial
criteria. The range of criteria, which are obviously to be considered, that
was just simply one of them. It's just that you hadn't mentioned it.
And it doesn't mean it's determinative in any way, but it is just one of those
criteria.
Thank you for that.
Okay.
Catch you all.
Thank you, and we'll move to Melissa.
Thank you very much, Madam Chair. Thank you for being here, Minister.
The Civic Information Service appropriation is the largest in the
vote with $246.2 million. Which
basically,
it's to fund the operations to produce identity products, I'm guessing,
passports and citizenship and all of that stuff.
But you actually talked about passports taking only three working
days and citizenship 50 working days.
How does that actually compare to when you became minister?
Oh, thank you for the question. Look, the department has gone
through quite a lot of
increase in volume. We are expecting
increase within the passport system because of the 10-year passport
coming into play,
and that taking effect for all the renewals this year.
As well as citizenship with the changes from labor
coming through this year as well with the
increase to residency pathways, now allowing people up to
five years to citizenship. So we're expecting an increase in both
sides.
So the department has known that this increase is likely to happen
this year and next year. And so we have been
prioritizing efficiency of service.
When I first came into office, however,
it was quite shocking to me some of the statistics
and the service efficiencies within particularly the
citizenship pathway. And so,
for the last six years,
the citizenship office has not met its targets.
However, for the last seven months we have
consecutively met targets of having
90% processed within 75 days. That had not happened for the
previous six years.
Okay.
And I'm really thrilled that the department
has met the message that we need to ensure that New
Zealanders, when they're accessing government services, receive
timely service, and that we have efficiency top of mind.
And they have responded very well to that.
One of the things that I asked of the department to do was
to start prioritizing older cases first because-
In one respect, it's very easy to try and meet your KPI of
having as many processed quickly as possible, but then
leaving the hard ones in the too hard basket.
And I was very annoyed and upset for individuals
whose files were coming across my desk at the
time when I encountered some that were years old, that had been sitting there in
the too hard basket. Doing both at the same time of increasing
the processing, which I'm happy for one of our officials to talk to
some of that in detail about how they have done that operationally,
as well as prioritizing those old backlog cases, has really ramped
us up to be able to be prepared for the surge in demand that will be
happening across both sides
of the citizenship and passport office.
But one area that has changed through this term of government has been
cross-skilling with the teams within passports and
citizenship, so that the teams can fluctuate
between working on just the citizenships or just the passports, but now
working on both to ensure that we actually have the right
people at the right time, keeping both of the wait times
low.
Supplementary to that. Just talking about how you have managed to
meet the target, did you set a different target, or was it the same target all the
time, and it's a method of how they operated that actually
increased the processing,
I guess, productivity? Or did you actually input
AI? Did you-
No
... What is the change that has actually seen this outcome?
Yeah. There's been a range of changes, and I'm happy for Hoani to talk to them
in more detail. We have used more AI.
We have also done more, like I say, of actually
trying to focus on clearing the oldest files first, which has
done an effect of bringing us up to having shorter wait
times.
But do we have an official that can talk to the specific operational functions?
Yes. I'll just ask Greg to come up, if that's okay.
Thank you.
And whilst he's making his way to the table. Good morning, everyone.
I'm Hoani Lambert. I'm acting for Paul James, who is the
secretary for DIA, and I'm the acting secretary
currently. So as the minister was saying, the
targets and standards for passports and citizenship have not changed.
The challenge that we had was actually meeting those targets as
a department. So,
the reduction in passport processing times has
been significant. So we were pushing up near 30 days three years
ago. We've managed to get that down now to three days.
Now, that's well below standard, and there's a few reasons for
that. Much of it is beyond our control, interestingly,
and that is because of the challenges we've had with the impacts of
conflict in the Middle East and what that has done in relation
to projected demand for passports.
It's much lower at the moment, and that's largely due to the
increased surge that we were anticipating being delayed.
We are anticipating that as things settle in the Middle East, and that there's a
way forward for the Strait of Hormuz, and then you eventually get
confidence building with New Zealanders to start considering travel again,
that we will see that surge start to manifest in our
processing numbers. The underlying driver for the
surge, of course, is the expiry of 10-year passports, which
began this year. We're expecting our
normal average of processing per month of around about
38,000, or 480,000 passports a year.
We were anticipating that to increase to around about
800,000 passports a year, or around about
70,000 passports per month on average.
We have seen an increase, so we've lifted up to 53,000 for May.
But we are expecting that once those surge numbers start to manifest,
that we will see the numbers of passport
applications coming through the system hit that 70,000 mark and that surge to
continue for around about four years through to 2030,
'31. Which means we've been anticipating a surge which hasn't quite
manifested, which means we've probably got more staff at the moment than we
had required. That has delivered a greater than anticipated
processing. But also, we have made some technological changes
and some improvements to our digital servicing.
And just whilst we're on passports, I'll get Greg to talk about those.
Yeah. So we've been running what we've been calling the Identity Services
Acceleration Program for-
Can you pull the microphone in?
Sure.
Yeah, so we've been running the Identity Services Acceleration Program for over 12
months, and so we've been focusing on just improving people, processes, and
systems. And we've been doing a lot of work retraining staff to just make sure
they're really competent. We've been looking to streamline processes, make them
more efficient, and we've been looking to improve our systems.
With citizenship, for example, in March 2025, we went live
with a major system upgrade, where we've automated the assessment of the
system end to end.
We've got interfaces with Immigration New Zealand, so we can API
status and presence information. We do a range of automated calculations.
And so we can actually assess entitlement upfront in an automated fashion
and then present those results to our staff if there's any exceptions, so they've
got a clear picture of what they need to do.
And we've seen significant backlog reductions because of that, so we're
definitely around 15% more efficient in processing
since we've gone live with those changes.
And as I've said, we've streamlined processes, and so we've just had a really big
focus on bringing that backlog down.
And at the moment, we've got about 10,000
citizenship applications in the queue, and that has been as high as almost 40,000.
So yeah, we're just a full court press in that space.
We've done the same thing in the passport space.
We've been really focusing on, again, clear automation where it's possible
And so adult renewals, we do a range of automated checking,
and if they are simpler applications, we expect those to process straight through,
so no human touch. That used to be sitting at about 28% of
adult renewal applications with straight-through process.
That's now sitting at 53%. And again, we've also done some things that,
better training of our people, better processes, aligning ourselves.
We ran a lot of capacity tests to just see how quickly we could process,
and process without error. And so we put those new processes into the
system to make sure that our people are effective, our processes are effective, and
of course, we've really tuned our systems.
We-- Sorry.
Last one because-
We've-
Too many
... been going a wee while.
Okay, too many questions. In terms of the automation and the AI
basically included in the processing, and that you're talking
about 100% fully automated, where does the accuracy, I
mean-
Yeah
... lie in terms of checking to make sure that the citizenship,
when people are actually given citizenship and getting New Zealand
passports, is actually accurate?
Yes. So there are still two human checks for all citizenship
application.
It's more of a rules-based assessment, and it pulls
information directly from authoritative sources.
So we actually pull information from Immigration New Zealand that confirms that
they are residents in New Zealand, they
have permanent residence, they've been here for five years.
So we do a lot of what we would call automated system checking.
Even with the criminal records and all of those checks that you're actually doing
already-
We have real time-
All of those are actually done.
Yeah. Yeah, we have real-time checks with the police, and they provide that
information, and of course, if there's no criminal convictions, these things come
through as all of these checks have been complete, and they are clear.
Our people then can make a simple assessment on that and move it through for
processing. However, if there are exceptions, then our people obviously
follow up with the police, handle those with customers, and then make
determinations that go through different processes for submission.
Yeah.
Okay, thank you.
Yeah, and just to follow up on that, if I can give a little bit of assurance that
I'm constantly receiving submissions that don't meet the
criteria as well,
mostly where it comes to people who have not clearly met the five years
or don't clearly meet good character requirements.
The minister individually must be satisfied that they do or
decline them, whereas for the majority of people, they don't have a
criminal conviction. They haven't done anything wrong.
They have been here for five years, and they do want to follow the law.
But for the exceptions, that's where a flag in the system is raised.
Okay, thank you. Limona.
Thank you. Thanks, Minister. I want to ask a question around FENZ and
then the vote,
3.492 million over the next two years.
Can you explain how
the estimated increased fuel costs will, over time,
impact on that budgeted vote? And also,
what has the price-- how did you get to that budget?
How well the workings in terms of that allocation for FENZ over the next
two years?
Yeah. Well, thank you very much. Look, I think everybody is aware that
we've been going through a bit of a crisis globally as well as here in New Zealand
with fuel shock. One of the things that the government has been
very clear on is that no matter what happened, whether we were at sort
of one, two, three, or four, it was crucial that our fire and
emergency system as well as all of our emergency
responders-
Mm-hmm
... had access to fuel suppliers to ensure that we
still had capacity around the country.
As part of the budget process,
I was asked by the Minister of Finance to put in a budget bid
for fire and emergency, and so for the particular numbers
of how much
was needed for fire and emergency, that came down to officials
from the Department of Internal Affairs as well as Fire and Emergency had
to come up with some specific numbers for how much extra they
thought they might need for added capacity.
However, that was also part of a Treasury-based analysis rather
than from my direct office. But I'm happy for Amanda here to
speak more to that.
Good morning. Amanda Moran.
So in the budget, there was 2.278 million included
for covering Fire and Emergency's additional potential fuel
costs.
And that, as was set out in the way that the minister's described, including
looking at general processes across all of government to make sure that there
was an equitable distribution of the money available to
help with the fuel crisis.
Thank you.
Yeah.
And just in terms of prioritizing those response activities within
the budget line with Treasury,
can you elaborate that two years? Do you think that that will be enough
based on those workings for FENZ in terms of that vote
specific?
Look, at this particular juncture in time, I do feel confident in that.
Clearly, we are dealing with a very dynamic global
situation.
I'm very happy to report even in this last week, it appears that, or at least
I shouldn't get too ahead of myself.
It appears that we have some form of consensus happening in
the Middle East,
where the strait will be reopened.
And it really does come down to what the fuel prices
will be doing day to day, week to week.
It is near impossible to forecast out-
Sure
... based on what will be happening globally.
However, I do know that Fire and Emergency did their best to
forecast out based on a range of different factors.
But how they came to those particular different factors is probably best
directed at Fire and Emergency officials themselves as to their own
assumptions that they built into how much did they want to request
from Treasury.
Thank you.
Okay. Lisa.
Just want to... Different topic.
Want to talk about, in your opening address, you actually talk about the
collaboration between the National Archives and the Library
Services on Anzac Stories.
Have they ever collaborated? Because most people would actually think that archives
are where you actually store old things, and library, basically,
it's a library. So what is this Anzac Stories
and what prompted that kind of collaboration?
Oh.
And are there going to be any other such?
Because often people don't think of archives and libraries.
Yeah. Well, thank you for the question.
Look,
I'm really thrilled by the work that archives and libraries have done in this
collaboration. They haven't worked on a digital collaboration
before, which is really what I was asking for as a minister.
One of the
reasons why was because I deeply
believe in the proper education of our next generations
coming through the schooling system, and one of the things that I'm very
passionate about is ensuring that we know about our history here in New Zealand.
And, I'm very thankful that for myself,
my mother has made me very aware of our connection to the wars,
and my great-grandfather fought at Gallipoli himself, and I've been very
aware of that story in our family. But I know for some
other members of our community, that's not the case, but they want to know how they
can be attached to this country and what our shared history is.
When it came to
delving into some of our national days, in particular for
Anzac Day, I asked our library as well as our
archive to work together,
on the sources that they had digitized to make them
accessible in a child-friendly way for the
next generations. And what's come out has been a really
good product. So Anzac Stories, which is featured
on the library's website, delves into
depths of individual stories that have been written
by a children's book author, then checked with war historians to
make sure they are factually accurate, using
footage as well as archival material from both library and
archives to build those collaborative stories.
Now, I know from some very early feedback that I've received, there's been
30,000 views of that particular page, which I understand is
much higher than we usually get on the National Library
website, for about eight minutes a session, which is also
very unusually long, which means that people are actually engaging with the
material. And I hope that over years to come, they will engage further.
But we do have our chief librarian here to talk further about that.
Morena. Rachel Essen, national librarian.
And just to add to the statistics that the minister's just
shared, the other thing is that it's driving people who haven't
previously used National Library and Archives to the site.
So approximately 54% of those
30,000 visits are from people who hadn't
been to the site before. But it's been the opportunity,
with the new heritage campus for archives and library
to work together more closely. This is a natural
progression of that, so we've physically come together.
We've got a new structure that brings us together organizationally,
and now that means that we're able to do a lot more,
and
produce things like the Anzac Stories.
This is intended as a pilot. We've learnt from how
to deliver that, and we will be looking at other topics
that we can deliver. And it was a really great co-design
process. We involved two local schools, Belmont School and
Adventure School in Porirua. And children from those schools
actually had an opportunity to model the
site and provide feedback and particularly suggest the
interactive
aspects of it, so the quizzes and things.
And they came to the launch, when the minister launched the site, and it was just
great. We also took the opportunity to show them physical
things from the collection. And other research has shown
that once you've engaged with something online, you're more interested in
seeing it physically. So it was great.
Looking forward to more.
Awesome.
You guys are ignored.
Yeah.
National Archives and the libraries.
That's right.
Wonderful work.
Thank you.
Andy, you want to jump on that?
Yeah, just a quick sup because I actually asked this question yesterday
when we had MCH in front of another committee.
And, it was a question about Ngā Tapuwae and the various different
interpretation that they do.
Mm-hmm.
But so thinking of your relationship together, Archives and the National Library,
is there any sort of cross-conversation?
I know MCH is not talking about a children's approach,
a children's specific education.
It's more about
general. But what level of collaboration is there or
should there be? Is that an opportunity to do things better?
And I know I specifically asked that question yesterday because some of our
historians are a little disturbed that we've kind of dropped the ball a little bit
after the World War I commemorations.
Yes.
In that area. Not so much, not here, but in that area.
Mm-hmm.
We definitely collaborate really well with our colleagues from
Culture and Heritage.
And we're very aware of the work that they're doing.
We've also been in discussions with them
to be able to archive any of their website or material
that they're not going to keep updating if that's decisions that are made.
So we work really closely with them to make sure that the overall
picture is preserved and available and accessible for New Zealanders,
wherever they might be.
So that sounds as if... Because the question which got raised, this was at a
fairness book town event with Chris Pugsley-
Mm-hmm
... Carter. And the question which was raised, who is the best
curator-
... the best guardian for all of this kind of material?
Because it did feel like MCH had kind of backed away a little bit
Um, and that's why I'll leave that question with you two.
I'd be interested in your answers-
Mm
... to whether in fact it is the National Library or the archives-
Mm
... that are perhaps the better curator for that MCH.
Well, we certainly operate as a system, as a heritage system.
We collaborate really closely with our colleagues at Te Papa Tongarewa-
Yeah
... and MCH, and right across Archives New Zealand and the
National Library both have system leadership roles, which we take really
seriously, and so we, uh, continually engage and work to support
each other and collaborate. Uh, I think there's always more that can be done in
that space.
So the door is open in terms of that, Rachel.
So did you want to talk about the role of the National Library in terms of
preservation-
Mm
... of websites?
Yeah.
Um, so that is something that
is part of our mandate.
Yeah.
So we collect and preserve information that's published,
and that includes online information and includes websites.
Uh, so we do a whole of domain harvest once a year, a
snapshot of everything. Uh, but we also have specific
websites that we preserve and collect and make accessible.
So if we asked, final question there, if we asked who the ultimate
guardian of these, um, uh, national memories
is, it really sounds as though it's the National Library more than anybody else.
Well, I think-
So if you saw others who were not doing what you thought they would do, you would
sort of-
Absolutely
... say, "Hey, you've got to do something about that," or alternatively-
Yeah. And our collection policy does state that-
Yeah
... is that, if it's of national significance,
uh, we're the repository for it. Um, the Turnbull Library
collections are kept in perpetuity.
Uh, archives collections are kept according to their, um,
uh, disposal authority and we take
that responsibility very seriously.
Mm. Do you think that's a common thread between Archives New Zealand, the National
Library, and the Alexander Turnbull, is that they are holding the documentary
heritage for New Zealand. And so making sure that these
institutions are working in an integrated way together in partnership with
other ministries like MCH is extremely important.
Yes, absolutely. And my vision for all of this, um,
work, starting with ANZAC stories, is to actually make it more accessible to
people because, you talk to an average man on the street, they can't

current financial year, there was just under 16 million
to support local government with natural hazards, which would
assist them to deliver effective local government services associated
with preventing or responding to adverse natural events or natural
hazards.
I can't see
that
in this upcoming financial year, and I'm just wondering why that's been
discontinued.
Can you just refer to the
page number on that?
The page number 91 for the vote internal affairs.
Right. Okay. So in regards to our broader reform
program around undertaking resilience activity,
there is a number of components that I have within my responsibility.
First and foremost, there is aspects around the broader flood
resilience work that
was previously undertaken by
Minister Mitchell, which is now transferred across to local government.
We do also have broader reform
and funding as part of the Auckland floods as well, which is a
part of that aspect.
So
there's a wide range of activity. That specific page number, I
haven't got it in front of me, but we can... Katrina, maybe.
A lot of the funding is time limited, so it was always time
limited for a specific purpose for specific regions.
On that 16 million, we'll come back
specifically with
where that sits.
But there is,
as I say, that 128.4 million going to insert
non-departmental funding, which is about
flood protection, cyclone recovery.
Yeah.
Yeah. Okay, we've got about 10 minutes left, so Andy Foster.
Yeah. So I want to have a look at an area we haven't covered yet, which is water-
Yes
... and delivery of the water service plans.
The advice we've got here is that the
DIA's initial analysis found that spending on water infrastructure will
increase by $9 billion. So I'm interested in if that
reflects a lot, $9 billion worth of extra work being done, whether you've
seen any efficiencies being proposed in what's being
done as opposed to, so are we getting more than it would've been, 9
billion buys more than 9 billion would've bought previously.
But that sounds like a significant uplift in what the councils were previously
planning to do. Very significant uplift.
And I'm also interested in, are there any concerns about
specific, and I know there are, specific water entities,
and the process for engaging with those water entities
to get them into a sensible shape. And I'd just
say, we had the Infrastructure Commission in front of T and I yesterday.
And they certainly were sort of suggesting that every
one of these water entities is all going to go out all together,
but all at the same time trying to do everything all at once, which will inevitably
mean competition for resources, pushing prices up, et cetera, and
possibly that it's unwarranted to do it that fast.
So, interested in your feedback on that to start with.
Yeah. I've got joined by Laurie Hand, who's the executive
director for water services, who will also provide some input
in regards to this. I mean, first and foremost, just to your point raised, I don't
agree with that statement that, in effect,
all these entities are going to come out at the same time and-
What was implied there is right, because actually, if you look at the facts,
Watercare in Auckland, which is one of the most significant water entities,
is already
in play and operating under this model and has been for a period of
time
and is already working with the market in terms of providing a forward look of
capital investment required over the next decade for
their needs and requirements. The savings or
the implications of the model of, in effect, allowing Watercare
to operate under Local Water Done Well
has had a positive impact in regards to what
was the forecasted increase of water charges under the previous
model. That was circa 27% increase,
which was obviously unaffordable under our Local
Water Done Well. It's in the region of 7%.
It'd be fair to say that now one of the challenges
before Local Water Done Well was is that we had, in effect, water
services across local government that was unregulated.
And we have the Commerce Commission now doing price
regulation, and we have Taumata Arowai, the water quality regulator,
doing water quality regulation. And that is very
positive. Obviously, we know the water quality issues
that we had historically, which is critically important for human health.
But having the Commerce Commission do economic regulation has been a
significant and positive step forward.
It also ensures that the Commerce Commission are able to review the
long-term asset plans of these entities to ensure that they
are, one, aligned to expectation of the needs of their communities, not
gold-plated. What I mean by not doing more than what is required
to meet the standards.
And I think that is fair to say that that has been an issue in the
past. But equally, there has been an impact of
increased costs of infrastructure flowing through, including from the Middle East,
that has flowed through, and you are seeing the price pressure of infrastructure
and water infrastructure
being under pressure as a result of a number of factors that
are lined up against the economy. So,
that's underway. If we stand back by the end of this term,
Local Water Done Well will be implemented.
We will be in a position where virtually all
councils will have a plan in play. We have
two councils currently, one with a facilitator, but one that has
submitted a plan to DIA for assessment, and the other is the Chathams,
which is currently working with the agency around their requirements.
We all know that they are unique in the context of their needs and
requirements, but all other councils have a plan and now are in
implementation.
And
in the main of challenge,
that is a positive aspect because we now have entities that
have the appropriate capability, experience, and expertise, including water
engineers, et cetera, managing and running these.
And these assets are within local ownership and control
and have professional governance and have a regulator around the outside of them.
So again,
feedback from opposition in the context of this is, is that
there may be a differing view around the pace and the
structure. But in terms of having a model that is
under regulation and economic regulation, I think it'd be a view that
is a better position than the previous state.
And just in terms of the spend and bringing that forward.
So I think what you were comparing was the previous 10-year long-term plans
to the water service delivery plans, and you're absolutely right.
There is more investment. So the Local Water Done Well legislation
requires financial sustainability, and part of that definition of
financial sustainability is investment sufficiency.
So what that is doing is requiring that where investment had not been made
and water services were not compliant, that that compliance
is now brought up to standard. So, for example,
in wastewater from 2022 to 2025, there were over
3,000 breaches
of consent conditions, which is doing damage to our environment.
As well, there's, I believe last count, 60 boil
water notices, long-term boil water notices in this country.
And that means there's our communities cut off without access to safe drinking
water. And so what Local Water Done Well is requires
those get remediated under investment
sufficiency.
And so that is what is bringing forward the investment to make sure that those
communities get the services they need to protect their health and their
environment.
What we're seeing, though, from the time the water service delivery plans
were submitted in September of last year to now when the water organizations
have a professional board in place and are really looking to
optimize that, we're actually seeing quite a bit of further optimization of those
plans. So one of them I can call out is He Awa, which is
Hamilton City and Waikato District Council.
In their water service strategy, they actually were able
to
continue their $3 billion of investment over 10 years but lower the
price signal. So they've sent in the water service delivery plan by
34% to
Hamilton City and by 59% to Waikato District Council
by just using some better sequencing of the network and really thinking through
that with some professional engineering standards.
Thank you. And look, just the other one is, are the-
Quick sap here and then a quick sap, Tony, if we can.
Sorry.
Yeah, quickly. So just, are there any entities that
are causing concern, and you're confident the regulatory regime that's
set up COMCO TA will do the job?
Yes. So we currently have, as I said, Waitaki
is one of the councils that have had a facilitator that I
appointed, that has subsequently submitted a plan through to
DIA for their assessment.
And the other council that's currently
working with the Department of Internal Affairs is Chatham Islands.
Chatham Islands. Yeah.
Outside of that, all of the other
councils in the country have their plans approved and are in the
process of implementation of their models.
In regards to
broader concerns and monitoring, as minister, I am keeping a
close eye on the number of water entities across
the board, including Wellington, which would
be one that has been historically
in the news and has been in focus-
Well, aware of that
... for a variety of reasons.
It'd be fair to say that in regards to that situation,
the Commerce Commission, which is the regulator, which has a role to
ensure, particularly around economic regulation, that that regulator is
actively involved in working with that entity alongside others
and providing me with regular updates in regards to that work and
progress. So I'm
satisfied that the regulation model that's currently in
play is doing what it is intended to do.
And we will continue to work with councils
constructively to ensure that this works.
We have to acknowledge that we are in a period of transition to a new
model from a state of governance around water
services that was different to what we currently have.
And that takes time to bed in and implement.
But,
in the main, what we're seeing is, as highlighted by a couple of the examples
in the Waikato alone and other entities, is actually
this is operating as expected.
And with more time as this beds in,
that will give positivity to ratepayers.
Importantly linked to the Head Start, it's another component of
the local government reform area is difficult, because
change and reform and amalgamation and consolidation is challenging,
as in any change process.
But it'd be fair to say the feedback that I've had from councils and mayors across
the country is that they have gained confidence and
capability through the water reform process that they are now
using to apply to the broader simplifying
local government process. And
I think that's an important aspect of building capability in local government and
leadership, that
having experience of amalgamation and consolidation cannot be
taken for granted. But the water reforms has equipped them better than not
having done so to progress reform in the future.
We've got a lightning-fast question to finish with.
Yeah. Are you satisfied that the Commerce Commission have enough
teeth to
do what they need to do?
I am confident in regards to the powers of the regulators to
undertake the activities required
to ensure that they are meeting their, or entities are meeting their
requirements. I meet with the regulator on a regular
basis and do
take feedback in regards to areas of opportunity to
improve and enhance that model. And as minister, I'm
always open to ensuring that our
regulators have sufficient powers and capability to
do their role, and importantly, to protect the New Zealand ratepayer consumer
and
I'll continue to work constructively with them in that area.
Thank you for your time today.
Thank you.
Thank you, committee.
And the committee will suspend until 10:30 when we'll be back to wrap things
up.
Thank you
